title: Conditional Probability Foundations
subtitle: Restricted sample spaces and basic conditional probability
intro: Conditional probability is the chance of an event given that you already know something else has happened. That extra information shrinks the set of possibilities to a restricted sample space — for example, once you know a card is red, only 26 cards remain in play.

You'll work with bags of marbles and a deck of cards, paying close attention to whether a draw is made with replacement (probabilities stay the same) or without replacement (the totals shrink, so later probabilities change). For two draws without replacement, multiply the first probability by the updated second probability.

Conditional probability is where intuition fails fastest in interviews: the sample space shrinks, and every count must be renormalized within that slice. Brainstellar-style card and urn puzzles exploit whether you treat draws as with or without replacement. Stat 110's mantra—P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B)—sounds trivial until a problem hides B inside a story about 'given that the card is red' or 'knowing the first flip was tails.'

Conditional probability is where intuition fails fastest in interviews: the sample space shrinks, and every count must be renormalized within that slice. questions.question: Two cards are drawn without replacement from a standard 52-card deck. Given that the first card is a heart, what is P(both cards are hearts)?

explanations.A: Correct. Given the first card is a heart, 12 hearts remain among 51 cards, so P(second is heart | first is heart) = 12/51 = 4/17.
explanations.B: 1/4 is P(heart) on a single draw, ignoring that one heart is already gone.
explanations.C: 13/52 equals 1/4, the unconditional single-draw heart probability.
explanations.D: 1/17 equals 3/51 and does not match the 12 remaining hearts out of 51 cards.

remediation.question: One card is drawn from a deck. Given it is red, P(it is a diamond) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Among 26 red cards, 13 are diamonds: 13/26 = 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/4 is P(diamond) unconditionally, before conditioning on red.
explanations.D: 0 would mean no red card is a diamond, which is false.

remediation.question: Drawing without replacement means the second draw has:
options: One fewer card in the deck than the first draw
The same deck size as the first draw
Independent probabilities from the first draw

explanations.A: Correct. The deck shrinks; later probabilities depend on earlier outcomes.
explanations.B: With replacement keeps the deck size fixed; without replacement does not.
explanations.C: Without replacement, draws are dependent, not independent.
explanations.D: Probabilities depend on the composition remaining, not a fixed 1/2.

questions.question: An urn holds 4 red and 6 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. Given that at least one is red, what is P(both are red)?

explanations.B: 2/15 is P(both red) unconditionally, not conditioned on at least one red.
explanations.D: 2/9 could come from an incorrect restricted sample space.

remediation.question: P(A|B) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Restrict to outcomes where B occurs and measure the fraction also in A.
explanations.B: P(A)/P(B) ignores the joint event in the numerator.
explanations.D: P(A)P(B) is joint probability under independence, not conditional probability.

remediation.question: Using complements, P(at least one red) in the urn problem equals:

explanations.A: Correct. 'At least one red' is the complement of 'both blue.'
explanations.B: P(both red) is a subset of 'at least one red,' not the whole event.
explanations.C: The first draw alone does not capture both draws.
explanations.D: Doubling a single-draw probability has no valid meaning here.
